student_message: Yes, the wings match. But I still don't get it.
final_message:
Math can definitely be hard sometimes, and it is totally okay to feel stuck! You did a fantastic job noticing that the wings match. Remember, feeling confused is just a normal part of learning, and I am so proud of you for trying! 🌟 

Let's try thinking about it like folding a piece of paper instead of using a mirror. Imagine you fold a piece of paper right down the middle. If you draw half of a heart on the folded edge and cut it out, what happens when you open it up? Both sides of the heart match perfectly! That fold in the middle is called a line of symmetry. It just means if you fold a shape in half, both sides touch perfectly, just like your own two hands when you clap them together. Does thinking about folding paper make it a little easier? 💛

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain reflection symmetry using a simpler real-world example to clear up her confusion.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Riya is focused but confused about the initial explanation. Dialogue will provide a simpler alternative explanation, while encouragement supports her confidence.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
